The good traders are just skipping the bad trade.
If you haven't done the work to create a strategy, a big part of a process is you have to take full
ownership of your trading. With a solid process for that first fact test, that's really the key.
Fact test takes a superpower. Fact test can really challenge some of your assumptions about the
market. A lot of times it'll just tell you you're flat out wrong.
Welcome to episode six of the Trading Floor podcast. I'm your host Garrett Drynan, I'm with the
co-host, the famous Tim Belden, and of course we always have Kurt in the background. So our

for a living. And this is somebody who has tested every indicator under their sun on tons of

I'm happy to be here. Yeah, I've been excited about this. And yeah, I think this is a continuation
of some of those coffee sessions we've had. We're not going to spend too much time on the bio,
but just to catch people up, right? You started as a discretionary trader. And then one day you
said, I'm going to stop following other people, right? I'm going to start building my own strategy,
as you transition from swing trading to day trading. You figured out how to back test where you

from doing this. And you developed a full process for developing these strategies, which we're going

like a discretionary trading. I was trading a system, but there was some definitely just some
discretion involved. And I thought I didn't do any back testing of it to that point. I've sort of

Let me just try it. So I had low expectations. And because I thought, hey, I've been discretionary
trading for a while. I've got this magic in my head. I can't possibly recreate that in code.
But the very first freaking back test I did was better than my discretionary trading. I was like,
oh my gosh, there's like a pretty humbling thing to see. But yeah, from that point forward,
I was like, okay, why am I why am I discretionary trading? Why is there any discretion at all when I
can just create a back test and do something that's better than that? It just didn't make sense to me.
So yeah, I've been systematically trading ever since then. Would you like to gain the biggest

To even add Garrett to your intro from all of those coffee chats and calls that we've done,
we've built out our entire client process. We learned everything we're doing from back testing,
analysis from those calls with you Dave, where I remember we would have the call and then immediately
we would get on and be like, how could we build this? Yeah. So everything we've learned even
extremely valuable to us. So I'm excited to jump in. It's really true. That's not an overstatement.
Not at all. So Dave, I've heard you say a number of times that when building a strategy,
it doesn't have to be unique. It doesn't have to be some esoteric secret thing that nobody knows
about. It can actually be something that's been done before. So I want to know what has led you to
believe this and to go by this philosophy. Yeah, it's a good question. So some of the first
strategies I started trading, I remember there was a vibrant blogosphere way back then,
early 2000s, and they shared ideas, shared trading ideas. There were some strategies that
people that traders sort of coalesced around. And I remember trading some of these strategies,
and I would check with some of the other traders that were trading it. And the variation between
the results was just wild. Like everybody's trading what they think is the same thing,
but everybody's having different results. And the answer or the reason for that is the good
traders are just skipping the bad trades. They have a system for like they're looking at all the
trades that come through and they have a better system for just figuring out ones they're going to
take and ones they're going to skip. So if everybody's looking at the same sort of base strategy,
the difference in the results is some traders are just skipping the right ones and others are not.
Some are taking those bad trades. So the better your optimization process,
like the less unique your initial signal has to be. So if you got a good enough optimization
process and you've got a good column libraries, I'm sure we'll go into. It really the signal,
like the main part of the strategy is like almost the least important part.
And so that what that means is it doesn't really have to be unique. You could get ideas from anywhere.
And like opening range breakouts, there's still some edge there. People look at those and
likely those don't work. But with the right combination, if you're skipping the right trades
through your optimization process, you can end up with a subset of those trades that's profitable.
So the better you are at that, like the more strategies you can come up with and create for yourself.
That's super interesting. I feel like everyone likes to say those simple strategies are overdone
and like burnt out. And you're saying, yeah, you just kind of have to make them your own and it all
goes into the process after the initial strategy. You know, I hear so many people say such and
such strategy is is gone. You know, nobody trades that anymore. I've got a strategy.
Some of the strategies that I've varied that I started trading in 2005, I still trade those.
And you would not like people, I haven't heard people mention the name of these strategies
in so many years. People long since thought they stopped working. But if you've got a good process,
yeah, you can you can get rid of the bad trades. Maybe you have fewer trades in the strategy now
than you did way back when. But you can still modify it to still have some edge with your optimization
process. Do you want to sit in on all of our daily and weekly in-house trader meetings?
There's no bigger edge retail traders can get. Visit smbtradingfloor.com to learn more.
So it sounds like the optimization process is like the magic to getting to nailing these things.
It's definitely the magic. And you know, that's what you know, a lot of traders just don't have
that process. They think backtesting is this a thing that is just different than what it should be.
And that's you know, with the with a solid process for coming up with a strategy for that first
back test, that's really the key. That's how you're going to end up with a strategy that's actually
profitable. But most traders, most traders that are backtesting are doing it the wrong way.
They're stuck in this guess and check loop. I call it the creative backtests. It doesn't quite
look good. They make some small adjustment based on vibes, you know, and they run another backtests.
And they're in this loop where they just keep guessing and checking and they never really
have a process for ending up with a profitable strategy. I want to dive into that first
blanket backtest more. But first like, what would you say for like any new traders? Is there like a
best strategy to start with trying to develop? Do you have any strategies that you think
yeah are the best ones to start with? I don't really point people to any specific strategy.
And you know, some people come to me and ask, hey, can you just give me your best five strategies?
Just the best five. I'm like, well, right. So the reason I don't give them that's not like
them selfish. But you know, if you haven't done the work to create a strategy, you don't really know
what you can't really have true confidence in it. So there's just going to come a point where
the strategy has a drawdown and you're not going to be able to have the confidence to trade through
it because why would you? You know, nothing really about it. So a big part of my process is
you have to take full ownership of your trading. And that means not looking over somebody else's
shoulder, doing it for, you know, coming up with your own ideas with collaboration with other
traders, you know, look for other ideas on that you see people talking about. But you really have
to make it your own to be successful and have a robust trading career that's going to last.
That makes sense. Someone gives me a Lamborghini and it breaks down. I'm going to have no idea how to fix it.
Yeah, I want to dive into this process for sure and pick your brand. I mean, hopefully we can get
everything out of you short of your social security number with that. But first, like, how do you
how do you come up with ideas? So like, do you usually have like an oh shit moment where you're like,
this is this needs to be tested or you just constantly kind of without excitement throwing
stuff at the wall to see like what your data says? Well, I'm putting myself in a position to notice
the unusual stuff that in the market. So, you know, it's you have to you have to have a routine for
looking at the market, looking at what has been moving that day and what's unusual about it. And,
you know, this process usually kind of steam rolls where new traders, they're kind of starving for ideas.
But once you start, you know, once you get an idea, once you get something that's working and
and maybe you get idea number two, all the sudden you have too many ideas. Like, at some point
during that process, it's not that you're starving for ideas. You have the opposite problem,
which is actually a bigger problem, like figuring out what to work on. But you really need to
put yourself in a position every day to notice the unusual things in the market because the
unusual things, those are the things that you could potentially create a strategy out of. So,
you know, at the end of the day, I'll look at certainly all the trades that my strategies traded
and I will have looked at those throughout the day and maybe something about them. I noticed that
I thought, okay, well, I haven't noticed that before. Do I have something about that captured in
my back test that I can see? Okay, how often does that happen? Sometimes it's the losing trades where
I think, okay, here's some unique situation. Maybe I could trade these the opposite way. Like, is
there some subset that I can use and just trade the opposite direction? So, I've got this sort of
laundry list that I have in my mind and, you know, and to do lists and such where I'm keeping track
of the ideas that I want to work on. And a lot of them just come from ideas I've already had
or strategies that I'm already trading or, you know, tangential ideas to the strategies I'm trading.
Like, I might see trades that almost met my criteria for a strategy, but didn't quite.
I'll have a process for looking at that because usually there's a lot of edge potentially there.
Like, if you think about the edges of your strategy or somewhat arbitrary, like, you sort of
come up with an arbitrary threshold, like just beyond that or just above it,
there could be potential profitable trades there. So, those are a good place to look for, you know,
new edges that you could create new strategies from. Could you give an example of that?
Where, because I think I know what you mean, but I'm not quite sure, you're trading a strategy and
you might be cutting out certain trades. And you're saying that beyond that point where you're
cutting them out, you might have trades like in the other direction because they're bad trades for
the strategies that, yeah, is that kind of what you're getting at? Yeah, so, so let me give you a
concrete example. One of the first strategies that traded was a gap strategy. So, it was looking
for gapping stocks above a certain gap percent. And then I would look for pattern in the first half
hour of the day. And basically, in a tight range and then on a break of that range, I would go
long. So, something gapping up, look for the tightening range, I would go long with a stop below
that range. So, what I noticed is sometimes those trades worked well, sometimes they worked poorly
and got stopped out. Some of them they worked horribly, like they would, I would get in and they
would, I would get stopped out in the stop and the stock would just tank, right? And I got to
think, and okay, well, I wonder, I wonder if there's some way I could just trade the opposite direction
for some of these. So, like, some of them weren't making my threshold, so I wouldn't, you know,
they weren't in as strategy I was trading at all, but I was watching them. So, I could see that
they were doing, they looked like there might be some edge in the other direction. So, I was okay,
well, let me create a strategy that does literally the opposite of what I was doing. And let me see
if there's edge there, like, can I create something where there's edge there? So, these are trades
that were, you know, the strategy was working, but I was, I thought, okay, there's a lot that I'm
just completely ignoring. Can I create a strategy that is completely additive to what I'm doing?
It's a completely new strategy that's completely isolated from an independent from the one I'm
already trading, so I wouldn't get like trades where I'm long and short in the same symbol. So,
I did that and created another strategy completely separate from that. So, now I have two strategies
really based on the same idea. That's one of the things that I like about automated trading,
because it might be not in your nature to fade a breakout and short it discretionarily,
right? Like, it just might not fit your personality, but you can kind of go into the data and then
check it out and see that it's a good strategy, automate it, and it doesn't really have to,
as long as you understand it and it has edge, like, it doesn't have to like fit your, you know,
mood for how you usually trade during the day. Do you agree with that?
I agree with that. I mean, a lot of times what you'll see is,
you know, back to us, it can really challenge some of your assumptions about the market.
And I mean, you, a lot of times it'll just tell you you're flat out wrong. Your intuition
is flat out wrong. For that reason, I think back to us, things are super power. Like, you see all this,
you know, gurus out on the internet saying things that, you know, these truisms about the market,
but, you know, you don't have to rely on what somebody's saying. You can actually go back test
and see for yourself. And a lot of what you, a lot of the stuff you hear out there is just
complete garbage. And if you've got a good process for testing that, it's, it is, it's like a
super power. I find it so interesting. So when you're saying like, you're putting yourself in the
position, what you're really doing is a ton of review work, looking at every trade you're taking,
looking at why it won, why it didn't win. And then what I even find more interesting,
because I've noticed with Garrett and I, you're seeing the ones where you're like, oh, I thought my
model would have taken Tesla today, but it didn't. Why didn't it? And then it's like, maybe you were
missing one of the columns that we'll talk about. One indicator was missing. But yeah, do you find
a lot of times that during the day or when you're reviewing at the end of the day, you're like, oh,
I thought I would have been in X, Y and Z totally. Yeah, that's a really good thing to be looking at.
And yeah, it's, I've got to really, that's a very common thing to, to need to be able to do.
Like go go and be curious about that. And you'll, like, there'll be some rule that you have in
your system that prevented it. But then you, you, you may brainstorm some way to be able to include
that, because you know, like, maybe Tesla today really was in the spirit of the strategy and what
your strategy is trying to do. And it might be worth trying to figure out a way to modify your
strategy so that Tesla's in there. So yeah, I think that's a great way to, I think that's a great
way to, you know, brainstorm and improve the edge you have and potentially create new ones.
I'm glad to hear that because that's my intuition. Like that's what I would want to be doing. Like,
okay, can we modify? But when I'm doing that, I'm like, is this wrong? Like they, they say you
shouldn't be like fitting it to one trade. You know, I'm, I'm not being a good quant right now.
I'm trying to, I'm trying to make my strategy catch this trade that I liked and, you know, maybe
that maybe there's no data to back it up, right? This is easy to overthink that stuff. Yeah,
I think I'm afraid, right? I think, you know, you hear the word, you know, curve fitting thrown
around a lot among systematic traders. And I think people are way too afraid of it. I think people should,
traders should really, it's not hard to avoid. And the biggest thing you can do is with your
back tests, start with a large number of trades, you're going to avoid a lot of issues with curve fitting.
If you, if you start from that point and I, yeah, I think there's, you know, I see so many traders are
kind of frozen due to fear of curve fitting. And I just think that I think you should,
traders should be more willing to make changes to the strategy. If you've got a good system,
a good process of reviewing, you're not going to, you're not going to be ruined by curve fitting.
And you're going to probably learn something by making those changes to your strategy anyway.

well, I'm backtesting an idea. The first back test should not be aiming for the final answer.
The first back test should cast a wide net. So what do you mean by that? And why is this so important?
Most traders are like, like I said, shooting for the final answer with that first back test.
And what you're going to, what you're going to end up with is something that
doesn't have as many trades as it could. And the trades aren't going to be as profitable as they could be.
And the reason for that is you're, by shooting for that final answer, you're sort of,
you're sort of adding your discretion, you're adding some of your assumptions in there. And
that's not really the right way to do it. So let's go back to my GAP strategy that I've mentioned
earlier. Gapping stocks up. I'm going long. You know, in my mind, I had an idea about what the
optimal threshold for the GAP percent would be. But so the first back test or two I did,
I was like shooting for that exact number. Let's say it was 5%. But then I thought, okay,
maybe I should be using a much lower threshold. That's what traders should do. Use a much lower
threshold for that GAP percent. So let's just say, let's say I assume it's going to be 5%.
I may do a back test on like 3%, which is going to have a lot more trades in there.
And that last minute is use optimization to figure out exactly what point makes sense for me to
use for that GAP percent threshold. So it's just a much better way to end up with, you know,
there's a lot of trades between 3% and 5% for the GAP percent that are profitable. And it allows
you to see that and include those in your strategy. So you may end up, I've worked with traders where
I've created like five times the number of trades in a strategy for them just based on doing this.
So it can be pretty dramatic. And it almost allows you to back test more with a more open mind
than most traders do. And I think that's very, that's helpful. And that helps you end up with
actually profitable strategies. And you get more data, more trades. That's always better for
optimization. So when you're saying the first blank at back test, you're casting a wide net,
you're lowering it to 3%. What else is in that back test? Like are you just buying on open? And
that's the only one indicator that you have in that first back test? So
well, with the, with this particular strategy, it trades, it's looking for that GAP and then it's
looking for a pattern after the after the open. So it's not buying on open, but the concept is
essential this same. So, so not only do I have GAP percent, but I add a whole bunch of other
columns to the back test that may or may not be predictive. And that's really the power of
optimization because you create, you create this column library of indicators that you include
with every back test. And that allows you to, for your tools to tell you what's important, rather
than you having to try to guess. I mean, I know my discretion for guessing for these things is
so bad, like I have to have tools that that tell me and then I can decide, okay, does that make sense
or not, not make sense? But yeah, it's just way easier to do it, to do it that way.
And the tools that just show you clear as day, too, that this is significant.
Yeah, and that's really how you learn. And that's how you're going to come up with a better
strategy tomorrow because your tools are essentially teaching you what makes your strategy work,
what makes it more profitable, what makes it bad. And any of those ideas that your tools
telling you can be fed into other ideas to create new strategies from.
Is there such thing as too wide of a net because I know like we've been in the position before

You definitely can go too far. It's a bit of an art. And I do see traders when they hear this

there's there's a bit of an art to it. The better starting point you have, like the better the

So yeah, I think there is a bit of an art to it and yeah, you can certainly do too many.
So I think it's like it's something you're going to have to play with and

What would I look at? What things would I need to see? What are the worst short trades? What

is not going to be this strategy in the end, right? And a lot of times, I mean, Tim and I'll run this,
you know, initial back test. And it's, you know, the P and L curve is straight down. That doesn't
phase us because we know that somewhere in there are probably our winning trades. And we just need to go
find them, right? And we find them by using what you call the columns in the back test. So,
if this is so important, what are the columns in the back test? Like, what do you mean by that? And
what are we doing with those? Yeah. So I thought of this coming from a software development perspective.
So, you know, it was a CTO trade ideas. I've done tons of development. It's a big part of what I do.
And the more, you know, when you write code, to be really effective, you need to write libraries.
So, shared libraries that other, you know, several projects can use. So, so you're not, you know,
when you create a new project or new software project, you're not just copying a bunch of code.
And you're using it over and over. You're, you set up a library for yourself so that you're using
this same library and that's shared across all the software projects. So, if you, if there's a bug
in the library, you can change it in this one place. And then all the projects that are using the
library are updated and, and made better. All at once. You didn't have to go make a change in every
single software package. And the same is, I treat my back test in the same way. So, I have this
column library that just sort of evolved from that very first back test of the gapping strategy.
I just, the first time I just brainstorm for different columns that I thought, or indicators
that I thought might be predictive. And then when I went on to strategy two, three, four,
I just kept using that same set of columns and adding to it. And what I realized is
the column library is like everything about what makes the strategy great. Like the better your
columns are, the more likely you're going to end up with the profitable strategy. And the more
powerful it is. So, over time, you've got this growing set of columns that have varying levels of
importance in different situations. And the bigger it is, the easier it is to come up with edge
in the future. And a lot of times when you work on strategy, you know, four, five, or six,
and you find something that's predictive there. Just by just, just observing the trades and realizing,
okay, well, this concept isn't captured in my column library. Like, for example,
yesterday's range for a stock has a pretty strong predictive value in a lot of situations
for today's trades. And putting that in your library as a like a standard column, all the sudden you
got another way to potentially weed out some bad trades and create more profit for your strategy.
So, the better your column library is, the better you're going to be as a trader. And when you
find something that you brainstorm for strategies five or six, find something that works there. All
the sudden, it might make sense to go back for strategies one through five and see if it has an
effect there. And it very, a lot of times you will find that it does. So, this is you're creating
for yourself this, just this constant improvement loop that just gets better and better and better over
time. And the same way that software library makes you better as a developer because you're essentially
leveraging your, your coding by doing that. The same can be true. When you take this approach
with back testing, your back testing gets better, just is leveraged and gets better and better,
and better, and better over time as you get more experience and figure out what is more predictive
in your strategies. So, you've been building your library since 2005. How many columns do you have now?
I've got probably 250, 300 somewhere in there. That's awesome. I work with some traders to develop
this type of process. And some of them have even more. I've got, there's one guy I think he's got
over 400. So, it's, I can totally see doing more. And if you've got, if you've got a good
process that for your tools that are telling you what's important, like there's no reason not to include
more of those in your library. Yeah, so we've been noticing that too.
Yeah, and this is my favorite part of testing these strategies because I like, sometimes you can get
a little bit creative because, right, because you're, you're talking about indicators, right? Like,
that's what these columns are. Yeah. And it's not just RSI and MACD and what some people assume,
you know, all indicators are, but like you mentioned, like yesterday's range. And when you start
to think about a strategy, you're thinking, not indicator first, you're thinking, what's,
what is this strategy? Like, what am I seeing in the market, right? And you might start to think,
well, this X, Y and Z seem to kind of happen. I wonder if I could measure that in some way.
And maybe I'll come up with a way to measure that and then that becomes a column. And now you're
kind of inventing indicators. And this is basically what we do in the Bionic Trader meetings as we go
over. Yeah. All these different ways to measure these market dynamics so that we can
A, test our strategies, B, maybe turn them into alerts or filters, you know, if they end up being
pretty good. So I want to ask you, if you were on a desert island and you could only have
three indicators to build the rest of your strategies forever. Okay. Which three indicators would
you choose? Wow. It's a really tough question. That's fun. My instinct is HCR is going to be up there.
Because I'm often sizing positions based on ATR, maybe placing a stop based on ATR
and flat out filtering based on ATR. So that's definitely going to be one of them.
Probably our vol, which, you know, relative volume. So the volume the stock is done today compared
to average. That's just going to tell you so much about what the, what the stock is doing.
It's hard to imagine not using that in a strategy somehow. The third one, that's going to be tough.
I'll have to think about it. I would say probably probably something like position in range.
That's something I use across different timeframes like yesterday. You know, what's the position
in yesterday's range? What's the position in today's range? What's the position in the last
10-day range? Like, so that general concept I think is, is a plug you can apply it in a lot of
different timeframes and situations. So that those are the ones that come to mind first.
I like it. In position in range, are you doing percentile or are you looking at like ATRs from
the yesterday's high yesterday's low or just percentiles? I was thinking of just percentile,
but yeah, like distance from those things in ATR, like the thresholds you said, distance from
open today in terms of ATR is really good. I mean, all these, these are all really good ideas
that you'll find popping up in your optimization process if you include them. Have you heard
this study of tens of millions of traders, which shows that only 1% of retail traders actually make it?
Don't be a statistic. Visit smvtradingfloor.com to greatly increase your odds.
Could you give an example of how one of these indicators, like let's just pick the position in range.
Like, how would that come into play in a strategy in terms of not only at showing edge in the data
providing edge for your strategy, but also making sense to you as a trader?
Okay, so go back to my original GAP strategy. So Gapping stocks up, looking for a pattern in the
first 30 minutes. So there's a, it's got a range. And if you're looking at the position in that range,
it's today's range. Values close to 100 mean it's close to today's high. And values close to zero
mean it's close to today's low when the signal happens. Well, those are very different situations,
right? So you might want to look at that to see, I mean, they're so different that surely when
you look at aggregate, there's going to be different edges there, like the ones that are close to the
top, I would assume probably going to be better than the ones close to the bottom, at least on the
long side. That would be my intuition. And if you look at the chart, it probably, I think that's
a way that traders can understand. And just describing that specific situation, you could see how
some of those trades are going to be very different. And you could use that to add a rule to your
strategy that improves it. So that's really what these columns are doing. All these columns are,
you know, you can think of them as indicators, but really what they are is opportunities for you
to create a rule to skip certain trades that are coming through the system. Like we said earlier,
you know, that's the difference between two traders that are trading what they think is the exact
same system. Some of them are just skipping the bad trades and taking the good trades. So
this, that's a really good example. I think it's probably crystal clear for traders. That's a good
situation to look for that, you know, one traders taking the ones at the top of the range and other
ones taking the ones at the bottom of the range too and the other one skipping them. So they end up with
very different results. So what tools do you use to determine after you run the back test and you
have these columns and you're able to look at all of these things like percent range and Rval?
What tool do you use to actually see that data and make the decision?
So I've created this tool for myself and you know, it's evolved at the years. Over the course of
15 years, I've developed a tool set for myself that I use locally. And when I realized just how
powerful it was for years, I was like, I'm not telling anybody about this that there's like,
it's too powerful. I'm not, I would be silly to do that. But I've, I've, I finally realized that
I come tried to make a big impact in this industry. There's so much snake oil. I see so many people
back testing the wrong way, like trading a strategy and they're so close, but you know,
if their optimization process was a little bit better, they wouldn't have quit. So I've made it
available as part of what I call made kit. It's called, it's the strategy cruncher. So basically,
you upload your back test and it's going to tell you exactly the optimal rules you could apply
to your strategy to make it better in a way that makes total sense to you. So yeah, that's the
tool I use and I used, you know, a more primitive version of it for many years and I, you know,
made it available as made kit for other traders to use. Thank you again for letting this use it,
you know, had the chance to dabble and it is very awesome. It's, it literally playing as they
just shows you exactly the indicators. Yeah, I know it's good just because I've used it for
so many years and it's great. And it's ridiculously fast. We tried to replicate a dumb down version
of it and it was a lot slower. Yeah. So you have the optimizer. It's telling you the exact rules
to use. Have you found there's like a magic number to the amount of rules that you want to put
then in the strategy? Is there too much rules to many rules? Yeah, so so a lot of traders when they
think of optimizing or optimization, they think, oh, well, curve fitting, you're going to end up
with too many rules like this is obviously curve fitting. But a good optimization process and when
you use the cruncher or some other tools, you're going to end up with fewer rules. So there's less
of a chance of curve fitting. So you know, it's going to tell you like back to our gap example,
it'll tell you the exact threshold to use. Maybe it's like 3.8 and a lot of traders will say,
oh, well, 3.8, you know, so specific, that's like curve fitting. Well, no, it's, it's literally
optimal point to use given your trading set. So there's no reason not to use that. Like the market
doesn't care that it's not a round number, right? Exactly. So I'm talking about. Yeah, so it's
like why not just use the optimal one? That's that's the one you should be using.
So yeah, how does that lead to fewer? You said now you're going to have fewer because you're using
more significant. Yeah, you're going to be selecting rules that are like more powerful, they're more
predictive. So when you can use one like why use three rules to filter trades down, when you can
use one that does all the work. And that's just going to make your strategy a lot less complex,
more easy for you to understand. And it's going to have, it's going to it's going to be more robust
and have more staying power when you do it that way. The thing I like about this strategy,
Crunchary, and just going about it this way is that when you run the back test, you have your
columns, right? You have your CSV file or whatever, and it's going to read that and then show you the
optimal cutoffs for all of these things, but it ranks them, right? And it shows the difference that
each indicator value, optimal value, makes in the back test. So you can see like these, you know,
these two or three seem to have a lot of predictive power and these are really robust. You know,
these other ones, they make it better, but it's not even close, right? And then you might notice the,
okay, these two or three are telling you something about the strategy, right? It's not just cool,
the numbers like check off the box. What's him and I like to do is say, oh, that's super interesting.
Now let's go look at the charts and see and pay attention to this particular market dynamic that
the strategy cruncher is telling us about and see if this makes sense. We add traders. Do you
think there's any value in bringing in that kind of sifting through charts discretionary sort of
element totally so you know, I, one thing that I did not do with the strategy cruncher,
which I totally could is like automatically come up with the entire strategy and all the rules.
But I purposely am only showing one rule at a time, like one set at a time. And I'm,
and that's on purpose. That's forcing you to think about which rule to apply. And that's really
how you learn. And in the exact process you, you, you just described of, you know, looking at what
it suggested, going back and look at the charts to see why that is. Well, why, why are these trades
looking like that? You know, why is this, why is it pointing to this value as optimal? Let me go
and look at some of those and see why. Oftentimes what comes from that is you're going to learn things
about your strategy and the market, which you didn't know before. And you're going to be able to
create a new strategy based on that knowledge. It's like completely separate from the one you're
looking at now. So really, the traders that use it here, here's the typical path is they,
they start using the cruncher, they find something to improve the strategy they're trading now.
That's great. They all the sudden they, they get rid of like 20% of the bad trades.
There's, there's strategies way better just from this one, you know, the first time they use it.
But the real power is strategy two and three. What's they realize? What power there is there?
You can think about a strategy from the ground up using this and really harness the power
so really strategy two or three are typically better because they've learned how it works.
They've learned something about the market that they didn't know before, some relationship that
is predictive and they created a strategy based on that. That's really where the power comes from.
We have our trade idea, right, our strategy idea with a, let's say a gap of trade, right?
And now we've developed the back test, casting a wide net, a lot of trades.
We have our columns, we have all of our indicators in there. We're now using the strategy
cruncher or whatever. This is the kind of thing where you can build these kind of things
yourself like Tim and I have. It hasn't been as good as the strategy cruncher. But, you know,
we're looking at the data and we're filtering out and now we have a really strong signal. So
great, we have a strategy but now we got to figure out where to sell. So how do you figure out how to
manage these trades and what's your process for that? So usually I've already decided that
by the time I run the back test. So I have some sense of it. And now the, so I might run a back test
without stocks or targets with just a time stop with the thought of picking the optimal stop to use.
And you can do that by using MFE and MAE, which are basically the, the excursions like the point
at which the trade was the most profitable and the least profitable. You can use that to figure
out the optimal stop to use. So what my typical path is create a back test with no stop or target.
Use that figure out what the optimal stop should be without, you know, on the unoptimized trade set.
Then run a back test with the optimal stop in there. So now I've got the, the,
the real trade set that I'm going to use and, you know, a subset of which I'll actually end up
going live with. And then I'll start the optimization process from that point.
And also one other option is to do some optimization without a stop. That's, and then compare and see
what things look important with it without the stop. That's another thing to do. It's more of an
advanced move. You could certainly sort of my, my default approach is select the stop, then do
optimization. Makes a lot of sense. On the target front, I remember from one of our calls,
you have quite the opinion on profit targets. What's your take on profit targets?
Well, is partial profits is what? Yes. That's good. You know, you hear one of the first things I think
you learn as a trader. You hear people saying, you know, if you're in a profitable position,
you know, what should you do? Lot of traders will say, hey, why don't you take a half off and move
your stop to break even? If you go back and do the math on that, it is one of the worst things you
could do. Like you're basically, if you compare that to not doing it, your equity curve is just way
higher when you don't take partial profits. And, you know, I found this out just by testing it and
doing that in my own trading. And then comparing to the back test, I'm like, man, I just, I just
gave up so much money by taking this partial profit. And, you know, it makes you feel better,
especially the break even too. You're like, oh, I'm not going to lose. You have to play with house
money, right? Yeah. But you're giving up so much by doing that. And a lot of it is just for psychological
comfort. Yeah. And which has some value, but at least understand why you're doing it. And,
you know, if, make sure you look at the numbers, if you were able to take your full size all the way
to the target, you're going to, there's going to be some really massive winners there. And
if you can stomach those, that's where a lot of your profits going to come from. So,
think about that. Go back and do the math and certainly understand the trade off about those
partial profits and what they're really costing you. And seeing that on paper too helps a ton.
You need to actually do the work and see it. And then you'll be like, oh, yeah, hate partial profits.
Adam Grimes told the story once. And I don't know if you guys are familiar with him, but he's,
he wrote that book, The Art and Science of Technical Analysis. And, really interesting guy.
I've read the book. It's very good book. And I heard him talk once about how he's a systematic
trader. I don't think it's fully automated, but he does tons of back testing and everything is
based on, you know, a system that ties processes. And for years, he was trading with the mentality of
holding and, you know, holding for big wins. And he started working with someone who said,
like, hey, Adam, I've been looking at all your trades. And it seems like if you just took a one-hour
profit on every single trade, you'd be doing a lot better. One-hour being like if you're risking,
you know, 10 bucks, then sell when you're up 10 bucks. And that, I mean, to a trader and Adam's
response was, there's no way that's going to be good. And that goes against everything I think
about as a trader. And of course, that's my reaction, too, is it's like, how can, you know,
you got to let your winners run? And he went back and tested it. And it was a lot better. And so,
I guess, like, from this point on, he's traded that strategy with the one-hour profit target,
like ever since, even to against his own intuition, just because that's what the data set.
What do you think about that? I think depending on the strategy that could be
a very good thing to do. I think it's going to depend on the signal. It's going to depend on the
spirit of what you're really trying to capture with the strategy. So, I would think ones that
trades where your whole time is shorter, maybe you're looking for a smaller move,
that would probably work really well. But if you're looking for something that needs to be
trending, you know, needs to trend all day for it to work, I would think a target further out,
or no target at all would probably be better. So, I think it's going to depend a lot on the
the strategy itself. There's not going to be one blanket answer that's going to work across every
strategy. Everything, every system is going to be different. And by no target at all, you mean sell
on the close? Right. That's something that, or cover on the close is something you do quite often,
correct? Yeah, I mean, a lot of, you know, I do exclusively day trading. So, if you think about it,
the longest whole time you can have is basically all day. So, a lot of times, I'm in positions all day,
and you know, if the day were longer, I'd be holding longer, but that's like a natural time to
to exit. So, yeah, often for a lot of strategies that trade, that's what I'm doing.
Speaking of days longer, Dave, are you thinking about the 24 or 7 markets? And
would that mean anything to you? Are you excited, maybe? Like, part of me thinks there might be
inefficiencies because it's new. And maybe there's some unusual activity, bring it back to what you
said, but yeah, I'm curious because that can be fun. Yeah, I think I suspect there'll be
some serious inefficiencies certainly at the beginning. I mean, there is some 24 or 7 trading now.
I think at IB, you can trade 24 hours with some names already. But yeah, I think there's,
you know, I think it's going to be a big change. I don't think it's going to be quite as big as
some people think, but I don't think the open and the close is going away anytime soon.
But yeah, I think it's certainly interesting. And anytime there's a big change like that,
there's always opportunities for edge. That's what I'm kind of thinking, at least, we'll
let those see. I think also Robinhood has the 24 or 7 trading right now. So I kind of wanted
to ask about, I know you've been working with a couple guys at SMB Capital, Memo and Chris Fs.
And I'm kind of curious how that started, that collaboration started.
Okay, so it's kind of an interesting story because I've always done a lot of writing,
just written stuff on a blog. I mean, starting from probably, it was probably 2000 when I started
doing that. And I started writing about trading in probably 2016 or 17 somewhere in there.
So I knew what I was doing was pretty unique. I mean, I didn't see anybody else doing what I was
doing. So I started putting some ideas out there. And all of a sudden, Bella, Mike Bellafore reached
out to me on Twitter. And he had read one of the posts I wrote and like wanted to meet up. We
had a Zoom call and he was asking about my trading and stuff. I was like, you know, I was
shared what I was doing. He said, oh, yeah, well, next time you're up in New York, let me know. Let's
meet up. So I was happy to be going up to New York in the next month or so. So I met up with him.
And I went over in detail, like I showed what I was doing, like all the trades I was making.
And I remember I was in his apartment there. We were looking at my trades. And one thing he said
was Dave, I don't think a lot of our traders could even get by with the rates you're paying
and I be and as well as you're doing. And I was like, oh, okay, well, I never really thought
about it that way. So basically, he, I think, saw something in me and it was all because of that
writing I was doing. It was, I'm sure it was 100% from the writing I was putting out there. And
so he said, why don't you talk with memo? And so yeah, I talked with memo. I met with him on
San Francisco. We got along really well. So, and so we've had this collaboration for some time now.
And you know, a big thing that Mike says a lot is networking is a trading skill and it totally is.
Like I've had many, I had, well, not many, but you know, a handful of collaborations over the years.
And you know, they come and go. Sometimes they're, sometimes they're productive for some period.
Sometimes they're not. But the more you share as a trader, the more you're going to get back.
And you should be, you know, a lot of traders are really secretive. They think they have
a secret sauce sort of like, you know, when I was sort of hoarding my tools 10 years ago.
You're going to get way more back when you share your information, building trust with other
traders. You know, you're not sharing things just willy-nilly, but really going out of your way to
and sort of systematically building trust with other traders to have collaborations.
And the way to think about is like, what can you give that trader that's going to make their life better?
You should be a default giver, not a taker. Most people are takers. Most people are thinking
about themselves. You should be thinking about how can you improve somebody else's life?
How can you make their trading better? And when you start thinking about that,
there's going to be ways you can help. And you'll develop these collaborations, which
they're going to be, you know, a lot of people, a lot of traders think about things in
completely different ways than you do. And they'll be able to share ideas with you that you will
literally never think of without that collaboration. So collaboration is like such an important part
of being a trader. And it's, you should have a process for building trust with other traders.
And part of that is being a giver, not a taker. That story is awesome. And Bella is the best at
networking. Yeah. So what makes the collaboration with Memo so good? Like what are each of you guys
kind of bringing? Like do you have a specialty that each of you are bringing to the team in the
collaboration? Okay, so most people when they think of who they can collaborate with, they're
looking for people that are basically clones of themselves, like that trade exactly like they do
or similar to they do it to the way they do. When really the better collaborations, you should
be looking for people that trade completely differently than you. And that was the case here. Like
they were doing stuff that I'd never thought about. And I was doing stuff that they had never
thought about. And it was completely different. So like the the process of sharing those ideas and
wrestling back and forth and you know, understanding why you thought this and why they think this.
And you know, you're obviously confident in this strategy. How did that confidence come about?
What was the path you took to get there? Those sort of collaborations are really where
magic can happen and you can really make big strides in your trading. So yeah, it's something,
you know, I have a very kind of specific process for that I suggest traders do and that's
you know, be a giver or not a taker. Yeah, it's a great advice. And that's where when you have
different skills in trading different things, one plus one equals a lot more than two.
Another bell a quote. No question. No question about it.
Everybody has an Achilles heel, right? Like every trader has a weakness, has a strength,
and sometimes when you find those partnerships where you kind of fill in the blanks a little bit
and help each other out, I think, you know, it's great. So I mean, Tim has no weaknesses,
which is why I partner with him. You're talking saying that in my forehead, but that's just a
selfish thing. So I'm just partnering with Tim for completely selfish reasons. But not at all.
I'll work on it. I've held you out a couple times, I suppose. Oh yeah.
Well, Dave, we got, we have one more question for you. And I'm going to ask it, but I'm going to
give Tim the credit for coming up with this question because I think that he should.
If you had a billboard in Times Square that you could put anything on, something you want the
world to see what would it be? Oh wow. If it was, if it was only traders in Times Square that
we're looking at it, what I would say is you're the strategy that you're going to make the most
money from in your career, you haven't thought of yet. I like that. So that message will be,
okay, what process are you going to put in place to create that, to put yourself in a position to
have the idea that's going to turn into a strategy that's going to be your most profitable? So that,
that's kind of the, that would be my message. I think that's, I think a lot of traders,
I work with a lot of traders that they don't realize how smart they are.
They don't realize how good they are and they don't realize how good they can be.
And I think that's my message to traders everywhere. You can be better than you are now.
And you've got it within you to do that. So let's do it. Let's do it.
Well Dave, thank you so much for joining us. It's been a pleasure, as always. We'll do this again.
I'm sure in the future, but we really appreciate it. Yeah, thank you, Dave.
Yeah, I appreciate you having me. Where can people follow you or find you?
Yeah, the easiest way is I've got a free course on back testing. It's called Better Back Testing.
So it's a six day email course. It's totally free. If you go to betterbacktesting.com,
you can sign up for it. It'll take you right to the sign up page. That's the easiest place to start.
I can also just feel free to email me at Dave at DaveMave.com is my email address.
Yeah, it's an honor to be on it, guys. This is totally fun.
Yeah, it was a blast. So we'll see you at the next coffee session.
Yeah, I'm excited to pick your brain again. Yeah, this is great. You guys are doing a great
job with this podcast. I was, I've listened every episode and you know, when you told me the idea
for this, I was like, well, that'll be kind of interesting. Let's see what it's like. But,
man, the episodes are riveting out of really like, listen to them. It's really great.
Thank you. Thank you. That's awesome. You're doing a great job.
Well, we're having fun. Doing it. We're having fun. We're good. We'll figure it out one of these days.
Exactly. All right, guys. Well, good talking to you. And until next time, trade well out there.

metadata.description:
Learn the top 3 trade setups we are using on the desk here: https://bit.ly/48TgJik

Spotify: https://open.spotify.com/episode/0m2HRiAvTBX5lrhMXpAAow?si=lEWiKcurSt-fFF0ejv7edA

In this episode of the Trading Floor podcast, we dive into the essential process that every trader must understand to avoid failure in their trading strategy. Host Garrett and co-host Tim engage with expert Dave Mabe, who emphasizes the importance of backtesting and developing a solid strategy. Viewers will learn the significance of taking ownership of their trading, optimizing strategies through data analysis, and the art of skipping bad trades, making this video a must-watch for active and day traders looking to enhance their execution and decision-making skills.

00:00 Why Most Traders Get the Same Results (and Why Some Don’t)
02:10 Dave Mabe’s Journey: From Discretionary to Fully Systematic
05:00 Strategies Don’t Need to Be Unique to Be Profitable
08:45 Optimization Is the Real Edge
11:20 Why You Must Build Your Own Strategy
13:45 How Dave Finds New Strategy Ideas
16:00 Turning Losing Trades into New Strategies
18:00 Backtesting as a Truth Serum
22:15 Why Your First Backtest Should Cast a Wide Net
27:00 Can You Cast the Net Too Wide?
31:00 Strong Signals vs Wide Exploration
37:40 The Power of a Column (Indicator) Library
44:05 If You Could Only Use 3 Indicators Forever
59:00 Profit Targets, Partial Profits, and What the Data Says
01:12:10 The Billboard Message Every Trader Needs to Hear


#smbcapital #stockmarket #daytrading

*SMB Disclosures* https://www.smbtraining.com/blog/smb-disclosures
